content: Would it be beneficial to include a direct link to the issue, or is it clear that it refers to this repository?
Direct links always please, the commits will be read from many places (including the terminal :), not just GitHub.
yes that is what I thought, should the Co-authored-by: Miguel Ojeda <ojeda@users.noreply.github.com> stay?
Not at all, remove that stuff that GitHub adds, please.

Also, for the link, typically you can put it in a tag like:

Link: https://github......

Can I also just use markdown syntax?

As seen in [#862] ...


[#862]: https://...
Should the link come before the Singed-off-by?

It is better to use the kernel tags. I always suggest to take a look at existing kernel messages, e.g.

commit 37934d4118e22bceb80141804391975078f31734
Author: Nathan Chancellor <nathan@kernel.org>
Date:   2022-08-30 22:34:07 +0200

    drm/amd/display: Reduce number of arguments of dml31's CalculateWatermarksAndDRAMSpeedChangeSupport()

    Most of the arguments are identical between the two call sites and they
    can be accessed through the 'struct vba_vars_st' pointer. This reduces
    the total amount of stack space that
    dml31_ModeSupportAndSystemConfigurationFull() uses by 240 bytes with
    LLVM 16 (2216 -> 1976), helping clear up the following clang warning:

      drivers/gpu/drm/amd/amdgpu/../display/dc/dml/dcn31/display_mode_vba_31.c:3908:6: error: stack frame size (2216) exceeds limit (2048) in 'dml31_ModeSupportAndSystemConfigurationFull' [-Werror,-Wframe-larger-than]
      void dml31_ModeSupportAndSystemConfigurationFull(struct display_mode_lib *mode_lib)
          ^
      1 error generated.

    Link: https://github.com/ClangBuiltLinux/linux/issues/1681
    Reported-by: "Sudip Mukherjee (Codethink)" <sudipm.mukherjee@gmail.com>
    Tested-by: Maíra Canal <mairacanal@riseup.net>
    Reviewed-by: Rodrigo Siqueira <Rodrigo.Siqueira@amd.com>
    Signed-off-by: Nathan Chancellor <nathan@kernel.org>
    Signed-off-by: Rodrigo Siqueira <Rodrigo.Siqueira@amd.com>
    Signed-off-by: Alex Deucher <alexander.deucher@amd.com>

Having said that, many times the usual

Bla bla bla [1]

[1] https://...

is used instead of Link, but I think it is best to use the Link if it is supposed to give important context.
Note that you can still refer to your Link in the text by putting the [1] after the URL

e.g.

    Link: 2ca4dcc4909d ("fs/mount_setattr: tighten permission checks") [1]
    Link: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/10374 [2]
    Link: Documentations/filesystems/idmappings.rst [3]
    Link: a65e58e791a1 ("fs: document and rename fsid helpers") [4]
how does the commit look to you now?

Looks much better, thanks! :)

Maybe use:

As seen in [1]

and add Reported-by: ... (@Boqun Feng?) if it makes sense.

Also, I would add the example from the issue inline of how it was unsound, if possible, i.e. in general, try to reduce as much as possible the need to read an external link -- that should be done by the reader only if they want to see more context, discussion, etc., but not to understand the change itself.

I am not sure if it makes sense, the original example in that issue is about forgetting the guard/using lock_no_guard. This is more of a general decision: can one thread lock and another thread unlock? I am not completely familiar with the kernels lock implementation, would that be a problem (one thread locks, another unlocks)?
@Miguel Ojeda please let me know if splitting the commits like this (in #903) is correct (do you have any conventions for merge requests?).

Splitting commits is usually better than opposite, as long as they remain logical changes and is reasonable, of course. Importantly, the kernel should still compile and work on each intermediate commit.

On the commits, some more nits I noticed:

- In the second, Reported-by would go before the Signed-off-by.
- In the third, the subsystem prefix is missing.
- Typically imperative mood is used (second and third).

They look quite good now in any case!

As for conventions for PRs, if you mean in our GitHub, nothing special apart from following the kernel rules for patches.
